answer:
In Allama Iqbal's poem *Khitab ba Jawanan-e-Islam* (Bang-E-Dara Part 3, ch. 13), he directly addresses Muslim youth ("O Muslim youth") starting in the opening lines of the main body and immediately contrasts their current state with the greatness of their ancestors (the early Muslim Arabs who conquered empires, lived with proud poverty, and created civilization—now lost through forgotten inheritance and books held in Europe).[^1]

> Have you ever,  
> O Muslim youth, paused for solemn reflection?  
> What was that  
> firmament, of which you are but a shattered star?  
> You were raised  
> in the embrace of love by that very nation  
> which once crushed  
> underfoot the crown upon the head of Darius.  
> Creators of civilization,  
> architects of the laws of empire,  
> they came from  
> the Arabian desert—that cradle of camel-herders.  
> ...  
> You can have  
> no true kinship with your forefathers,  
> for you are all talk, they were all action; you are static, they were ever-moving.  
> We have lost  
> that inheritance we received from our ancestors;  
> ...  
> But those pearls of knowledge,  
> those books of our forefathers—  
> when I see them now in Europe,  
> my heart shatters into fragments.[^1]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 13 (*Khitab ba Jawanan-e-Islam*), [p-365462](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/13-khitab-ba-jawanan-e-islam/translation/#p-365462). (English translation; full poem is 2 paragraphs / 268 words.)
